candidate_problems.text: How can the conjecture be operationalized into a falsifiable comparative framework that distinguishes 'interaction structure' from formal constitutional design, leadership quality, and background socioeconomic conditions? The central unresolved issue is whether the proposed causal variable can be measured independently and shown to predict survival under stress rather than redescribing successful cases after the fact.

What specific feedback mechanisms convert high-stakes conflict into revisable disagreement, and under what conditions do the same institutions flip from negative to positive feedback? This is an open problem about causal microstructure: which combinations of parties, media, courts, bureaucracies, civic associations, and coercive institutions actually absorb loss, repair trust, and bound escalation, and when do they instead amplify defection.

How does constitutional interaction structure adapt when new social cleavages emerge that were not anticipated by the inherited regime, without making adaptation itself appear regime-breaking? The unresolved tension is between flexibility and legitimacy: systems must revise rules to metabolize new conflicts, but revision can also signal that prior guarantees are contingent and trigger existential struggle.
